title: Extracting WPS resources beyond the arbitral award
primary_topics: Arbitral Award (alternative paths to maritime rights)
sub_topics: China's 2009 Note to UN Secretary General
archipelagic state entitlements (TS, CZ, EEZ, ECS)

stances.claim: China will not budge on the Arbitral Award because acceptance would force it to (1) allow freedom of navigation, (2) dismantle SCS installations, and (3) abandon 'historic rights' taught as essential heritage.

Even without invoking the Arbitral Award, the Philippines can argue that China impliedly accepted Philippine sovereign rights in the WPS — China's 2009 Note objected only to the Spratlys/Scarborough sovereignty claim, not to our LOSC-recognized entitlements in TS, CZ, EEZ, and ECS.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal argument from implied acceptance + textual reading of China's own Note

stances.claim: Resource extraction can proceed through constitutionally permitted modes — co-production, joint venture, production-sharing with 60%-Filipino entities, or FTAA with foreign corporations under broad presidential discretion per La Bugal-B'laan v. Ramos.
stances.rhetorical_move: constitutional enumeration + citation of his own jurisprudence

stances.claim: The Philippines should extract WPS resources before science renders them valueless — green substitutes for minerals, petroleum, and other mineral oils are coming.

cases: Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China (2016 Arbitral Award)
laws_treaties: UNCLOS / Law of the Sea Convention (1982)
events: China's diplomatic Note to UN Secretary General (April 13, 2009)

signature_phrases: Ah, I will leave them to the negotiating skills
decision_framework_signals: applies implied-acceptance doctrine + textual reading of adversary's own diplomatic statement as alternative path to disputed legal claim
frames constitutional resource-extraction modes as a menu of operative options for the President
uses face-saving framing for China (avoid invoking the Award explicitly) as a diplomatic strategy
uses technological-obsolescence urgency to justify near-term action
